questions.question: Does this review explicitly reference mechanical pretreatment (e.g., grinding, milling, extrusion, shearing, chopping,...)
Does this review explicitly reference thermal pretreatment (e.g., microwave, hydrothermal treatment, steam explosion, autoclave)
Does this review explicitly reference ultrasound pretreatment, the litterature sometimes uses synonyms such as sonication, ultrasonic,...

Does this review explicitly reference pressure-based pretreatment (e.g., high-pressure homogenisation, pressure shock treatment, hydrodynamic cavitation, mechanical jet), (Methods where pressure is only indirectly applied due to enclosed heating (e.g., autoclave) must be excluded.)
Does this review explicitly reference pulsed electric field or other electrical pretreatment techniques applied to a substrate to disrupt material and increase availability
Does this review explicitly reference alkaline pretreatment
Does this review explicitly reference acidic pretreatment
Does this review explicitly reference oxidative pretreatment (e.g., traditional oxidizers such as H₂O₂, or advanced oxidation processes such as oxidizers combined with UV, Fenton reactions, or TiO₂)
Does this review explicitly reference solvent-based pretreatment

Does this review explicitly reference chemical pretreatment, where chemical pretreatment signifies the degradation of complex material to improve bioavailability using some chemical compound that is different from the more prevalent techniques (alkaline, acidic, oxidation and solvent)? Examples of those techniques can be (surfactant, solubilizer, salts,...)
Does this review explicitly reference enzyme-based pretreatment (this also includes crude enzyme treatments)
Does this review explicitly reference fungi-based pretreatment (this excludes enzymes extracted from a fungi)

Does this review explicitly reference microbial-based pretreatment (this can be a bacterial treatment but also microbial consortium) (note: enzymes extracted from a microorganism as well as the use of substrate rich in microorganisms should be excluded)
Does this review explicitly reference biologically active substrate for pretreatment, (this can be through the reuse of digestate, adding compost, rumen, or activated sludge,...)

Does this review explicitly reference nanoparticles to improve the digestion process(can be for various ways, generally to improve methane, but through different mechanisms: nutrients, electron transfer,... for example

Does the article describe the use of disinhibiting treatments (e.g., coagulants, biochar, ammonia stripping) applied to the substrate or reactor before or during digestion to improve microbial activity or methane production
Does the article describe the use of chelating agents added to the substrate or reactor before or during digestion to enhance microbial activity or substrate degradation

Does the article describe the addition of nutrients (e.g., nitrogen, phosphorus, micronutrients) to the substrate or reactor before or during digestion to improve microbial activity or methane production during the digestion process

Does the article describe additional treatments applied to the substrate or reactor before or during digestion to optimize the digestion process, (considering treatments that do not have as goal the improvement of bioavailability of material like you would have with physical, chemical, or biological treatment), treatments such as nutrient addition, disinhibition with: coagulants, biochar, ammonia stripping,..., chelating agent or the use of nanoparticles but excluding these ones other similar treatments, it can be simple things such as washing in algae to remove salt for example
Does it mention the batch reactor as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention the SBR (sequencing batch reactor) reactor as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention the CSTR reactor as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention the Leach Bed reactor as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention the plug flow reactor as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention then Anaerobic Contact reactor as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention the UASB reactor as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention then EGSB reactor as an anaerobic digester

Does it mention the UASB derivate reactor (excluding EGSB) as an anaerobic digester, this infers a sludge blanket reactor such as IC reactor (internal circulation), induced bed, upflow solid reactor,...
Does it mention or describe the baffled reactor as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention or describe the filter/fixed bed reactor (a reactor with a settled biofilm) as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention or describe the fluidized bed reactor (similar to filter/fixed bed with biofilm except that the support media is in suspension) as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention or describe the derivative of biofilm reactors (such as filter/fixed bed or fluidized bed but excluding these ones specifically) as an anaerobic digester
Does it mention or describe the membrane reactor as an anaerobic digester

Does it mention or describe the hybrid reactor as an anaerobic digester, where here hybrid reactor is considered as a mix of two reactor designs (example UASB with fixed bed inside), this category should exclude reactors called hybrid but that are two or more reactors put into series (example fixed bed stage + fluidized bed stage)

Does it mention or describe the multistage reactor as an anaerobic digester, where here multistage is defined as two or more reactors as multiple stages (for example leach bed reactor in combination with high rate reactor such as UASB, or multiple CSTR in series), care needs to be put into consideration in reading the text appropriately for this question, as certain researcher might call their reactor hybrid even though it is only reactors put into series.

Does it mention or describe small scale reactors as an anaerobic digester, here defined as low tech reactors with no heating or mixing, such as fixed dome, floating drum or tubular reactors (usually made out of plastic, the text needs to make it clear that it is such a low tech reactor)
Does it mention or describe the lagoon reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as a large covered reactor with no mixing and often no heating

Does the article explicitly describe the use of any anaerobic digester reactor type not listed here: (batch, SBR, CSTR, Leach Bed, plug flow, anaerobic contact, UASB, EGSB, UASB derivatives, baffled, filter/fixed bed, fluidized bed, biofilm derivatives, membrane, hybrid, multistage, small-scale, lagoon, rotating disk/contactor, gradual concentric chambers, tower, loop, hydraulic flush, bionic, labyrinth flow, self-flotation), where the text clearly explains the specific reactor design and its operation anywhere in the main text?

Does it mention or describe the rotating disk or rotating contactor reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as a reactor in which one or more disks rotate within the reactor volume to promote attached and suspended biomass growth through periodic contact with the substrate

Does it mention or describe the gradual concentric chambers reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as a reactor composed of concentric zones or chambers enabling sequential anaerobic (and potentially aerobic) treatment within a single structure

Does it mention or describe the tower reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as a vertical, modular reactor in which biogas-induced circulation or gas-lift effects promote mixing and biomass suspension, including tower or multiplate configurations

Does it mention or describe the loop reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as a reactor incorporating a physical loop that recirculates the substrate through the system, typically driven by biogas lift or mechanical means to enhance mixing and mass transfer

Does it mention or describe the hydraulic flush reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as a semi-continuous mixed reactor in which a portion of the digestate is periodically removed, phase-separated, and partially returned to the reactor to reduce inhibition effects and control process conditions

Does it mention or describe the concept of a bionic reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as a reactor or process design inspired by biological digestion systems, such as animal gastrointestinal tracts, potentially involving multi-stage configurations or bioaugmentation strategies

Does it mention or describe the labyrinth flow reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as a compartmentalized reactor with a complex up- and down-flow path designed to separate or control acidogenic and methanogenic phases, similar in operation to a baffled reactor

Does it mention or describe the self-flotation reactor as an anaerobic digester, defined as an upflow reactor in which biogas bubbles are intentionally used to attach to and float suspended solids for separation or retention within the system
Does it, in that context, explicitly describe the use of the digestate as a fertilizer
Does it, in that context, explicitly describe the use of the biogas directly as an energy source, this means research around biogas performance/advantages/disadvantages for electricity, cooking,...
Does it, in that context, explicitly discuss the biogas adoption subject around social issues
Does it, in that context, explicitly discuss the biogas adoption subject around economical issues
Does it, in that context, explicitly discuss the biomass potential of a substrate or a geographic area
Does it, in that context, explicitly describe the enhancement of digestate as a fertilizer through nutrient addition
Does it, in that context, explicitly describe the enhancement of digestate as a fertilizer through drying
